coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 Chinese Society of Particuology and Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Chinese Society of Particuology and Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Xiangyi
Feng, Yali
Li, Haoran
Du, Zhuwei
Teng, Qing
Wang, Hongjun
coredata.dc:description: To investigate the effect of nitrogen on the photoluminescence properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal treatment using biomass tar as the carbon precursor. As an inevitable organic pollutant, the unsaturated bonds in biomass tar, such as carboxylic acids, aldehydes, and aromatics, are favorable for formation of the graphitic carbon lattice. The obtained N-CQDs are spherical with an average particle size of 2.64nm and the crystal lattice spacing is 0.25nm, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon. The N-CQDs emit bright blue photoluminescence under 365nm ultraviolet light, and they have excellent water solubility and stability with a high quantum yield of 26.1%. Coordination between the functional groups on the N-CQD surface and Fe3+ ions is promoted because of the improved electronic properties and surface chemical reactivity caused by N atoms, leading to a significant fluorescence quenching effect of the N-CQDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions with high selectivity and sensitivity. There is a linear relationship between ln (F 0/F) and the Fe3+ concentration in the N-CQD concentration range 0.06–1400μmol/L with a detection limit of 60nmol/L, showing that the N-CQDs have great potential as a fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon quantum dots
Fe3+ ion detection
Fluorescence quenching
Biomass tar

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N-doped CQDs and undoped CQDs
Detection of Fe3+ using the N-CQDs
Dependence of the FL emission intensity of the N-CQDs on the pH
Highly sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of the N-CQD–Fe3+ system

item-info.ce:copyright: Chinese Society of Particuology and Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the N-CQDs. The insert shows a HRTEM image. (b) Size distribution of the N-CQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of (1) biomass tar and (2) N-CQDs (b) XPS spectrum of the N-CQDs, high-resolution XPS spectra of (c) C 1s and (d) N 1s of the N-CQDs.

(a) PL excitation and emission spectra of the N-CQDs sample. The insert shows the samples under the illumination of sunlight (left) and UV light (365nm, right). (b) FL emission intensities of the N-CQDs samples under different excitation wavelengths.
Regression curves of the FL integrated area versus absorption at 360nm.
Fluorescence intensity versus pH of the N-CQD solution.
(a) F 0/F versus pH in the presence of Fe3+ (500μmol/L). (b) F/F 0 values before (black) and after (white) addition of Fe3+ (1000μmol/L) in the presence of different metal ions.

(a) FL spectra of the N-CQDs (20μg/mL, pH=4.0–4.5) with different Fe3+ concentrations (from top to bottom, 0, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000, 1200, and 1400μmol/L) at 340nm. (b) FL emission ratios (F 0/F) of the N-CQDs (20μg/mL) in the presence of different Fe3+ ion concentrations.
Regression lines of ln (F 0/F) versus Fe3+ concentration at 340nm in different N-CQDs concentration solutions: (a) 8, (b) 12, (c) 16, and (d) 20μg/mL.
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of the N-CQDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions.
Recovery results of acid leaching wastewater using the proposed method and atomic absorption spectrometry.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Civil and Resource Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Civil and Resource Engineering
University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Biochemical Engineering, Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Biochemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To investigate the effect of nitrogen on the photoluminescence properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal treatment using biomass tar as the carbon precursor. As an inevitable organic pollutant, the unsaturated bonds in biomass tar, such as carboxylic acids, aldehydes, and aromatics, are favorable for formation of the graphitic carbon lattice. The obtained N-CQDs are spherical with an average particle size of 2.64nm and the crystal lattice spacing is 0.25nm, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon. The N-CQDs emit bright blue photoluminescence under 365nm ultraviolet light, and they have excellent water solubility and stability with a high quantum yield of 26.1%. Coordination between the functional groups on the N-CQD surface and Fe3+ ions is promoted because of the improved electronic properties and surface chemical reactivity caused by N atoms, leading to a significant fluorescence quenching effect of the N-CQDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions with high selectivity and sensitivity. There is a linear relationship between ln (F 0/F) and the Fe3+ concentration in the N-CQD concentration range 0.06–1400μmol/L with a detection limit of 60nmol/L, showing that the N-CQDs have great potential as a fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Conventional semiconductor quantum dots, such as CdO, CdS, and CdSe nanostructures, are usually severe health and environmental hazards because they contain heavy metals (Magdalane, Kaviyarasu, Vijaya, Siddhardha, & Jeyaraj, 2016). Thus, green, highly efficient, and eco-friendly nanostructures need to be discovered to replace semiconductor quantum dots. As a new promising carbon material, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted increasing interest since they were discovered in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004). CQDs have a lot of promising applications, especially in metal ion detection, cell imaging, and biosensing (Dong et al., 2012; Evanko, 2005). Compared with semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs have attracted more attention because of their excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, functionalizability, nontoxicity, good availability, facile synthesis, and low cost (Gnach & Bednarkiewicz, 2012; Kaviyarasu, Manikandan, & Maaza, 2015; Lai et al., 2016; Li et al., 2012). Several methods have been developed for synthesis of CQDs, including laser ablation (Li et al., 2013), microwave irradiation (Chandra et al., 2012), solvothermal treatment (Hsu & Chang, 2012), and pyrolytic degradation (Chandra et al., 2011; Dong et al., 2010). To enhance the quantum yield of CQDs, functionalization of CQDs with passivation agents, such as ethylene glycol polymers, leads to a significant increase in the fluorescence (FL) emission efficiency, especially doping with N atoms (Wang et al., 2010). The one-step hydrothermal method is the traditional way to prepare CQDs because of its advantages of facility and low cost, Zhou, He, and Huang (2013) reported a one-step hydrothermal method to prepare CQDs using natural peach rubber. Liu, Zhou, Li, Lei, and Yan (2016) used chocolate to obtain water soluble CQDs by the one-step hydrothermal method and applied them to Pb2+ detection.

Fe3+ is one of the most widespread metal ions used in industrial and agricultural processes and it plays an important role in many physiological and pathological processes, including cellular metabolism, enzyme catalysis, and oxygen transport (Hentze, Muckenthaler, Galy, & Camaschella, 2010; Lynch, 1997). Current detection assays, such as voltammetry, spectrophotometry, atomic absorption spectrometry, and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, require sophisticated instrumentation and tedious sample preparation procedures (Liang et al., 2007). Thus, it is still a great challenge to develop a simple method for Fe3+ ion detection. FL assays have the unique advantages of high sensitivity and selectivity, simplicity, and easy monitoring.

Carbon precursors, such as glucose, sucrose, citric acid, and ascorbic acid, have been used to prepare fluorescent CQDs, but most of the preparation processes require multi-step operations and post-treatments with passivation agents to improve the water solubility and photoluminescence (PL) properties (Zhai et al., 2012). Biomass tar is an organic pollutant and an inevitable pyrolysis by-product in smelting processes using biomass, and it contains harmful organic substances, such as carboxylic acids, aldehydes, and aromatics. The rich unsaturated bonds in biomass tar are favorable for formation of the graphitic carbon lattice of CQDs (Deng et al., 2017). In this study, we synthesized N-CQDs by hydrothermal treatment in a single step using biomass tar as the carbon precursor and the small organic reagent ethylenediamine, which is easy to purify. FL spectrophotometry for Fe3+ detection was established according to the FL quenching effect between the as-prepared N-CQDs and Fe3+ with high selectivity and sensitivity.

The biomass tar used in the experiments was obtained by condensation and rotary distillation of the pyrolysis products of wood chips, which were generated in a nitrogen atmosphere at 500°C. Ethylenediamine was purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). A variety of metal ions were introduced by addition of soluble metal salts (NaCl, MgCl2, KCl, CaCl2, MnCl2, Fe(NO3)3, FeSO4, CoCl2, NiCl2, CuCl2, ZnCl2, CdCl2, BaCl2, and HgCl2). All of the reagents used in this work were of analytical reagent grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-doped CQDs and undoped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment. In brief, 10mL of 10% biomass tar and 1mL of 0.1g/mL ethylenediamine solution were transferred into a 50mL stainless steel autoclave equipped with Teflon and then placed in a muffle furnace at 180±2°C for 2h. After cooling to room temperature, the dark-brown solution containing N-CQDs was collected by filtering the resulting solution with a 0.45μm filter membrane to remove the generated graphite particles, followed by drying at 80°C for 6h to remove excess ethylenediamine. The obtained N-CQDs were dispersed in a 100mL volumetric flask containing distilled water for characterization and use in the experiments. The undoped CQDs were prepared under identical conditions without addition of ethylenediamine.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were performed with a JEM-2100F microscope (JEOL, Japan) with a field-emission gun operating at 200kV. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption and FL spectra were recorded with a UV-1750 UV/vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and a F2700 FL spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with a infrared spectrometer (T27-Hyperion-Vector22, Bruker, Germany). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed with an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ using the N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of Fe3+ was performed at room temperature. A certain amount of N-CQD solution was dispersed in distilled water. A calculated amount of Fe3+ was then added, the pH of the sample was adjusted to 4.0–5.0 by adding HCl and NaOH, and the solution was diluted to 50mL with distilled water (pH=4.0–5.0). The FL quenching effect on the N-CQDs was investigated by measuring the FL intensity of the samples at different Fe3+ concentrations. The sensitivity and selectivity measurements were performed in triplicate to ensure the reproducibility of the method.

A solution of quinine in 0.1mol/L H2SO4 (literature quantum yield of 54% at 360nm) was used as the standard. The quantum yield was calculated using the following equation (Hu et al., 2009):
(1)
ψ =
ψ
R
I / A
I R
/
A R
(
η
η R
)
2
,
where ψ is the quantum yield, I is the integrated area under the emission spectrum, A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, and η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript R denotes the standard (quinine sulfate).

The TEM image (Fig. 1 (a)) reveals that the obtained N-CQDs are spherical with high dispersibility. The HRTEM image (insert of Fig. 1(a)) shows that the crystal lattice spacing is 0.25nm, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon (Dong, Yang, Ding, Wei, & Hu, 2015). The size histogram and Gaussian fitting curve (Fig. 1(b)) show that N-CQDs have a relatively narrow size distribution ranging from 1 to 5nm with an average particle size of 2.64nm.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded to identify the functional groups of the biomass tar and N-CQDs (Fig. 2 (a)). The peaks at 3421, 3147, and 1643cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH, CH, and CO bonds (Jia, Li, & Wang, 2012; Kwon & Rhee, 2012). The peaks at about 1400cm−1 are because of the CC stretch of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, revealing that the biomass tar contains harmful organic substances, such as carboxylic acids, aldehydes, and aromatics. These unsaturated bonds in the biomass are favorable for formation of the graphitic lattice structure. In the spectrum of the N-CQDs (Fig. 2(a)(2)), the peaks in the range 1400–1600cm−1 are assigned to CC bonds, confirming formation of the graphitic lattice structure of the N-CQDs. The peak at 1500cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of CN bonds, and the peak at about 1230cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of CN bonds, indicating that N is fixed to the surface by addition of ethylenediamine. The peaks at around 1043cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of CO bonds. According to the infrared spectroscopy analysis, the obtained N-CQDs contain hydrophilic groups (OH, COOH, and NH2).

To further investigate the chemical environment around the carbon and nitrogen atoms of the as-synthesized N-CQDs, XPS was performed. Fig. 2(b) shows the survey spectrum of the N-CQDs. The peaks at 284.28, 398.75, and 530.39eV correspond to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively (Edison et al., 2016). This result indicates that the N-CQDs are mainly composed of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen. From the high-resolution scan of the C 1s region (Fig. 2(c)), the four different peaks in the C 1s spectrum are assigned to sp2 carbon atoms of CC (284.5eV), sp3 carbon atoms of CN (285.2eV), CO (286.8eV), and CO/CN (288.6eV) (Yang et al., 2014). From the high-resolution scan of the N 1s region (Fig. 2(d)), the peaks at 398.9 and 399.8eV can be assigned to CNC and CN groups, respectively. The FT-IR and XPS characterizations show that the surface of the as-synthesized N-CQDs is functionalized by multiple oxygenated and nitrous groups, leading to excellent water solubility for subsequent detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution.

The PL excitation and emission spectra were recorded to investigate the optical properties of the obtained N-CQDs. Fig. 3 (a) shows that the optimum excitation and emission peaks are at 340 and 420nm, respectively. The insert of Fig. 3(a) shows optical images of the N-CQDs under sunlight and UV light (365nm) illumination. The bright blue PL of the N-CQDs is sufficiently strong to be easily observed with the naked eye. Fig. 3(b) shows the FL emission spectra under UV–Vis excitation for wavelengths from 300 to 400nm. The FL emission intensity first increases with increasing excitation wavelength from 300 to 340nm and then decreases with increasing excitation wavelength from 340 to 400nm. The maximum FL emission intensity at the excitation wavelength of 340nm can be ascribed to n–π* transitions of CO bonds (Cao et al., 2007; Ming et al., 2012; Zhou et al., 2007). The FL emission peak gradually shifts to longer wavelength (from 409 to 463nm) as the excitation wavelength increases (Wu et al., 2013). This could be because of the different light selectivity of different N-CQD sizes (Liu, Yang, & Bai, 2016).

Fig. 4 shows that there is a linear relationship between the integrated area (I) and absorption (A). The I/A values of quinine sulfate, N-CQDs, and CQDs (undoped) are 95.0×105, 45.9×105, and 19.9×105, respectively. According to Eq. (1), the quantum yield of N-CQDs (26.1%) is much higher than that of undoped CQDs (11.3%), demonstrating that the FL emission intensity of the CQDs can be significantly enhanced by doping with nitrogen. Formation of pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen atoms caused by N-doped carbon dots can break the large conjugated carbon structure, leading to stronger fluorescence emission (Qian et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dependence of the FL emission intensity of the N-CQDs on the pH
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5 , the FL emission intensity of the N-CQDs slightly increases with increasing pH from 2.0 to 4.0, and then significantly decreases with increasing pH from 4.0 to 8.0. The amino groups of the N-CQDs, such as pyridinic nitrogen atoms, are gradually protonated as the pH decreases, and introduction of protons from the protonated nitrogen to the large conjugated carbon structure enhances FL emission. Because of proton saturation of the nitrogen sites in the N-CQDs, the FL intensity remains almost constant for pH<5.0.

The enhancement of FL emission caused by protonation of the amino groups could be because of two possible reasons: (1) Formation of hydrogen bonds resulting from protonated amino groups could effectively prevent electron transfer in the excited state and facilitate recovery of the fluorescence of local emission states (Qian et al., 2014). (2) The protons could modulate the electrostatic interactions between the N-CQDs (Hu, Zhao, Chang, Yang, & Liu, 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Highly sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The ratio of F 0 to F can reflect the fluorescence quenching effect, where F 0 and F are the FL emission intensities of the N-CQDs at 340nm without and with Fe3+ ions, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 , the FL emission intensity significantly decreases with addition of Fe3+. The relationship between F 0/F and the pH in the presence of Fe3+ (500μmol/L) is shown in Fig. 6(a). The F 0/F values at pH=4.0 and 5.0 are much larger than those at pH=2.0 and 3.0, indicating that a better fluorescence quenching effect can be obtained in the N-CQD–Fe3+ system at pH=4.0–5.0.

Fluorescent carbon nanomaterials can be used to detect metal ions because of their different effects on FL emission (Qian et al., 2012; Zhou, Lin et al., 2013). The effect of different metal ions on the FL quenching selectivity coefficient (F/F 0) of the N-CQDs (20μg/mL) was evaluated by adding different metal ions (1000μmol/L) (Na+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, and Hg2+) to N-CQD solution. As shown in Fig. 6(b), only Fe3+ significantly quenches the FL emission of the N-CQDs (F/F 0 =0.0922), indicating the high selectivity of the N-CQDs for Fe3+ detection in aqueous solution. The fluorescence quenching effect can be detected by adding Fe3+ in the presence of possible interference metal ions. The fluctuation of the F/F 0 value is very small, indicating that N-CQDs have good anti-interference ability for detection of Fe3+.

The feasibility of Fe3+ ion detection was investigated by measuring the FL emission intensity at 340nm for different Fe3+ concentrations. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the FL emission intensity of the N-CQDs (20μg/mL, pH=4.0–4.5) sharply decreases with increasing Fe3+ ion concentration, indicating the possibility of Fe3+ ion detection using N-CQDs as a fluorescent probe. Because of insertion of N atoms, the electronic properties and surface chemical reactivity improve (Li et al., 2014). The relationship between the FL emission ratio (F 0/F) and Fe3+ ion concentration shows an exponential relationship (Fig. 7(b)) rather than a linear relationship, as has been reported in previous studies (Jiang, Han, Jin, Zhang, & Wang, 2015; Wu, Jiang, Gu, & Tong, 2017), indicating that the Fe3+ detection sensitivity of this fluorescent probe is higher than other reported CQDs.

The regression curves of ln (F 0/F) versus Fe3+ concentration at 340nm in different N-CQD concentration solutions are shown in Fig. 8 . Ln (F 0/F) shows an excellent linear relationship with the Fe3+ concentration in the N-CQD concentration range 0.06–1400μmol/L. The linear regression equations are ln (F 0/F)=0.10469+0.00222[Fe3+] ([N-CQDs]=8μg/mL, R 2 =0.9963), ln (F 0/F)=0.07577+0.00225[Fe3+] ([N-CQDs]=12μg/mL, R 2 =0.9966), ln (F 0/F)=0.07346+0.00225[Fe3+] ([N-CQDs]=16μg/mL, R 2 =0.9969), and ln (F 0/F)=0.08012+0.00225[Fe3+] ([N-CQDs]=20μg/mL, R 2 =0.9963). The equation has a high degree of consistency for N-CQD concentrations from 8 to 20μg/mL. The amount of added N-CQDs has no effect on detection of Fe3+. Therefore, simple operation for Fe3+ detection can be achieved and the average equation is ln (F 0/F)=0.08351+0.00224[Fe3+]. The detection limit of Fe3+ based on 3δ/S, where δ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and S is the slope of the linear calibration plot, is estimated to be 60nmol/L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of the N-CQD–Fe3+ system
ce:section.ce:para: When Fe3+ ions were added to as-prepared N-CQD aqueous solution, the FL emission intensity was significantly quenched. As shown in Fig. 9 , this strong quenching effect may originate from formation of N-CQD–Fe3+ complexes. The functional groups (e.g., phenolic hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups) on the surface of the N-CQDs can interact with metal ions to form coordination complexes (Li et al., 2014; Qian et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2013). Introduction of N atoms (the electronegativity of N is 3.04), which act as electron donors, is crucial for tuning the electronic local density of the N-CQDs and promoting coordination between Fe3+ ions and the functional groups on the surface of the N-CQDs, facilitating annihilation of non-radiative electron/hole recombination and leading to electrons in excited states transferring from the surface of the N-CQDs to the half-filled 3d orbital of the Fe3+ ions (Dong et al., 2012; Qian et al., 2014; Wu, Li, & Yan, 2009). Thus, a significant fluorescence quenching effect is observed in N-CQD aqueous solution containing Fe3+ ions.

To investigate the applicability of the N-CQDs, recovery tests of acid leaching wastewater were performed by the standard addition method. The measurement performance of this method was compared with the atomic absorption spectrometry method. The Fe3+ concentrations of the real sample determined by the proposed method and atomic absorption spectrometry are 62.8 and 63.4mmol/L, respectively. Using the proposed method, the recovery of Fe3+ ranges from 96.6% to 102.1% and all of the relative standard deviation (RSD) values are less than 1.2% (see Table 1 ). According to the results of the atomic absorption spectrometry method, the recovery of Fe3+ ranges from 94.8% to 102.9% and the RSD values are less than 2.1%. These results confirm the accuracy and feasibility of N-CQD probes for highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ in real samples.

In this study, CQDs were prepared from biomass tar and N-doped with ethylenediamine by one-step hydrothermal treatment. The as-prepared N-CQDs emit bright blue photoluminescence under 365nm UV light and exhibit excellent water solubility and stability with a high quantum yield of 26.1%. The obtained N-CQDs are spherical with an average particle size of 2.64nm and the crystal lattice spacing is 0.25nm, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon.

Because of the improved electronic properties and surface chemical reactivity caused by N atoms, a significant fluorescence quenching effect of the N-CQDs is observed in the presence of Fe3+ ions with high selectivity and sensitivity. Ln (F 0/F) has a good linear relationship with the Fe3+ ion concentration in the N-CQD concentration range 0.06–1400μmol/L, and the linear regression equation is ln (F 0/F)=0.08351+0.00224[Fe3+] with a detection limit of 60nmol/L, showing its excellent practical feasibility.
Conflict of interest
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Major Science and Technology Program for Water Pollution Control and Treatment (2015ZX07205-003), the China Ocean Mineral Resources Research & Development Program (DY125-15-T-08), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21176026, 21176242).
